run.prompt: Please review MSA-Helios-Cloud.docx from our position as the CUSTOMER and produce a redlined version with surgical edits protecting us: cap our liability at 12 months' fees, and narrow any unilateral supplier rights. Batch all edits into a single apply_redline call. (Environment note: run tool calls strictly one at a time — never issue parallel document searches; this sandbox has tight memory.)
run.final_answer:
Redline applied to **MSA-Helios-Cloud.docx** — saved as **"MSA-Helios-Cloud (redlined).docx"** for you to download and accept/reject.

---

## What was redlined (4 edits, 9 tracked-change regions)

| # | Clause | What changed | Why |
|---|--------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | **5.1** — Liability cap | `liability of Helios` → `liability of **either party**` | Makes the 12‑month‑of‑fees cap mutual, so **our** liability is capped at the same level as Helios'. |
| 2 | **4.3** — Suspension rights | Added **10‑day notice‑and‑cure** before Helios can suspend for overdue charges; added "where immediate suspension is reasonably necessary" for AUP‑breach suspension | Helios could previously suspend "on notice" instantly — a cure period protects against inadvertent payment delays, and the reasonableness qualifier prevents disproportionate service cuts. |
| 3 | **7.2** — Feedback / improvements | Helios **no longer owns** Customer feedback; instead Customer grants a **non‑exclusive, royalty‑free license** to use feedback only to improve the Services | The original was a unilateral IP grab — any suggestion the Customer made became Helios' unrestricted property. |
| 4 | **1.2** — Acceptable‑use / fair‑use policies | Policies locked to **Effective Date version**; **30‑day notice** required for any material change | Prevents Helios from changing the rules of use unilaterally without Customer knowing. |

---

## What was NOT redlined (needs your commercial judgment)

- **Clause 4.1** — 24‑month initial term with **no termination for convenience**. That's a significant lock-in, but it's a commercial decision (early‑exit cost vs. commitment price) rather than a unilateral legal right.
- **Clause 1.3** — "As available" basis with no SLA. If you expect uptime commitments, an SLA schedule should be added.

---

## Items requiring human judgment

1. **Clause 4.1 lock‑in**: Do you want to propose an early‑termination right (e.g. Customer may terminate for convenience after 12 months with 60 days' notice, paying only accrued fees)? This is a negotiation point.
2. **SLA / uptime**: The MSA has no service‑level commitment — only "commercially reasonable efforts" on an "as available" basis. For a 24‑month commitment on a CRM platform, an SLA (e.g. 99.5% uptime with service credits) is standard in enterprise SaaS.
3. **Order Form**: The MSA references an Order Form for pricing, term length per subscription, and service scope. Do you have it? If so, I should check for conflicts between the MSA and the Order Form.

steps.summary:
1	---
     2	name: msa-review-saas
     3	description: Use when the user uploads or pastes a Software-as-a-Service Master Services Agreement (MSA), Master Subscription Agreement, SaaS Agreement, or Cloud Services Agreement and asks for review, redline, risk assessment, or recommendation on whether to sign. Conducts a structured review of MSA framework terms (liability, indemnification, IP, data protection, warranties, term and termination, payment, and others), calibrated to the user's perspective (vendor or customer), with severity-rated findings, redline language for material gaps, and clause-level citations. Optionally surfaces conflicts between the MSA and a provided Order Form or SOW.
     4	lq_ai:
     5	  title: MSA Review — SaaS
     6	  version: 1.0.0
     7	  author: LegalQuants
     8	  tags: [contracts, msa, saas, subscription, review]
     9	  jurisdiction: US-default
    10	  trigger_examples:
    11	    - "review this SaaS MSA"
    12	    - "review this software subscription agreement"
    13	    - "redline this cloud services agreement"
    14	    - "what should I watch for in this MSA"
    15	    - "is this SaaS contract okay to sign"
    16	  inputs:
    17	    required:
    18	      - name: document
    19	        type: document
    20	        description: The SaaS MSA to review (PDF, DOCX, or pasted text). The skill assumes the document is the framework agreement; Order Forms and SOWs are optional supplements.
    21	      - name: perspective
    22	        type: text
    23	        description: Which side the user represents. One of "vendor" (the SaaS provider supplying the service; we want strong limitations of liability, broad acceptance of our terms, customer payment obligations, and operational flexibility) or "customer" (the SaaS customer subscribing to the service; we want strong service commitments, controllable termination rights, data protection, IP ownership of customer data, and reasonable liability allocation). If not provided,…

steps.summary:
[MSA-Helios-Cloud.docx (45 pages) — full text]

HELIOS CLOUD LTD — MASTER SERVICES AGREEMENT (Vendor Standard Form)
This Master Services Agreement (the "Agreement") is made between:
(1) HELIOS CLOUD LTD, a company incorporated in England and Wales (registered number 09182736) whose registered office is at 40 Helios House, 12 Meridian Street, London EC2A 4TP ("Helios", the "Vendor", "we" or "us"); and
(2) NORTHWIND TRADING LTD, a company incorporated in England and Wales (registered number 07654321) whose registered office is in London ("Customer", "you"),
each a "party" and together the "parties". This Agreement takes effect on the date of last signature (the "Effective Date").
1. PARTIES & SERVICES
1.1 Helios operates a cloud-hosted customer relationship management platform (the "Platform") and shall make the Platform and related support available to the Customer as a service (the "Services") in accordance with this Agreement and the applicable Order Form.
1.2 The Services are provided on a subscription basis for the Customer's internal business use only. Helios grants the Customer a non-exclusive, non-transferable right to access and use the Services during the term. All use is subject to the Platform's published acceptable-use and fair-use policies.
1.3 Helios shall use commercially reasonable efforts to make the Platform available, but the Services are provided on an "as available" basis save as expressly stated in this Agreement.
2. TERM AND RENEWAL
2.1 This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and continues for an initial term of 24 (twenty-four) months from the Effective Date (the "Initial Term").
2.2 On expiry of the Initial Term, this Agreement shall automatically renew for successive renewal periods of 12 (twelve) months each (each a "Renewal Term"), unless either party gives the other written notice of non-renewal of at least 90 (ninety) days before the end of the then-current term. The Initial Term and each Renewal Term together form the "Term".
3.…

     6	  version: 2.1.0

     9	  jurisdiction: US-default
    10	  trigger_examples:

    22	        type: text
    23	        description: The house positions / playbook tier to apply (preferred / fallback / walk-away). If absent, apply the standing in-house posture and flag anything at or below a walk-away floor for the supervising lawyer.
    24	---
    25	
    26	# Surgical redline craft — change the few words that need changing
    27	
    28	You are marking up the **other side's** draft with native tracked changes, via `apply_redline` (and
    29	`preview_redline` to check your work first). A redline is read by a human who accepts or re…

steps.name: update_matter_memory
steps.summary:
{"content_md": "# Matter: F081 Live Verify Probe — Helios Cloud MSA

## Parties
- **Helios Cloud Ltd** — Vendor (SaaS CRM platform). Counterparty. UK company (England & Wales).
- **Northwind Trading Ltd** — Customer (our client). UK company.

## Document in play
- **MSA-Helios-Cloud.docx** — Helios' standard-form MSA, redlined from Customer perspective.

## Redline completed (4 edits applied)
1. **Clause 5.1 — Mutual liability cap** — Changed "liability of Helios" to "liability of either party", capping Customer's liability at 12 months' fees (matching Helios' cap).
2. **Clause 4.3 — Suspension cure period** — Added 10-day notice-and-cure before Helios can suspend for non-payment; added reasonableness qualifier for AUP breach suspension.
3. **Clause 7.2 — Feedback IP** — Converted Helios' full ownership of Customer feedback to a non-exclusive license to improve the Services.
4. **Clause 1.2 — Published policies** — Locked AUP/fair-use policies to Effective Date version; added 30-day notice for material changes.

## Open items
- Clause 4.1 — 24-month lock-in without termination for convenience (commercial, flagged but not redlined).
- No SLA / uptime commitment (document says "as available" at 1.3 — may need SLA if this is an enterprise deal).
"}

steps.name: update_matter_memory

---

| # | Clause | What changed | Why |
|---|--------|-------------|-----|
| 1 | **5.1** — Liability cap | `liability of Helios` → `liability of **either party**` | Makes the 12‑month‑of‑fees cap mutual, so **our** liability is capped at the same level as Helios'. |
| 2 | **4.3** — Suspension rights | Added **10‑day notice‑and‑cure** before Helios can suspend for overdue charges; added "where immediate suspension is reasonably necessary" for AUP‑breach suspension | Helios could previously suspend "on notice" instantly — a cure period protects against inadvertent payment delays, and the reasonableness qualifier prevents disproportionate service cuts. |
| 3 | **7.2** — Feedback / improvements | Helios **no longer owns** Customer feedback; instead Customer grants a **non‑exclusive, royalty‑free license** to use feedback only to improve the Services | The original was a unilateral IP grab — any suggestion the Customer made became Helios' unrestricted property. |
| 4 | **1.2** — Acceptable‑use / fair‑use policies | Policies locked to **Effective Date version**; **30‑day notice** required for any material change | Prevents Helios from changing the rules of use unilaterally without Customer knowing. |

---

## What was NOT redlined (needs your commercial judgment)

- **Clause 4.1** — 24‑month initial term with **no termination for convenience**. That's a significant lock-in, but it's a commercial decision (early‑exit cost vs. commitment price) rather than a unilateral legal right.
- **Clause 1.3** — "As available" basis with no SLA. If you expect uptime commitments, an SLA schedule should be added.
